content: He was punctual, dressed in black, with crape around his hat, and presented himself at his cousin's with a face made up for the occasion, and which he could alter as might be required.
Where is her stepmother?
"Who is he?"
Is he a deputy?"
"Have you mentioned this death in your paper?"
But whom are you seeking, Debray?"
"I think he is about to leave Paris; he was going to his banker."
"His banker?
"Still, he ought to have been here," said Debray; "I wonder what will be talked about to night; this funeral is the news of the day.
But hush, here comes our minister of justice; he will feel obliged to make some little speech to the cousin," and the three young men drew near to listen.
The banker saw the carriage of the count enter the court yard, and advanced to meet him with a sad, though affable smile.
"Well," said he, extending his hand to Monte Cristo, "I suppose you have come to sympathize with me, for indeed misfortune has taken possession of my house.

Do you know, count, that persons of our time of life-not that you belong to the class, you are still a young man,--but as I was saying, persons of our time of life have been very unfortunate this year.
"What new calamity?"
"My daughter"--
"The truth, my dear count.
Oh, how happy you must be in not having either wife or children!"
"Do you think so?"
"Indeed I do."
"The other night she left."
But still, we have quite lost our dear Eugenie; for I doubt whether her pride will ever allow her to return to France."

Philosophers may well say, and practical men will always support the opinion, that money mitigates many trials; and if you admit the efficacy of this sovereign balm, you ought to be very easily consoled-you, the king of finance, the focus of immeasurable power."
Danglars looked at him askance, as though to ascertain whether he spoke seriously.
"Yes," he answered, "if a fortune brings consolation, I ought to be consoled; I am rich."
There was a moment's silence, during which the noise of the banker's pen was alone heard, while Monte Cristo examined the gilt mouldings on the ceiling.
"Are they Spanish, Haitian, or Neapolitan bonds?" said Monte Cristo.
Stay, count," he added, "you, who may be called the emperor, if I claim the title of king of finance, have you many pieces of paper of this size, each worth a million?"
"To the Governor of the Bank.
Please pay to my order, from the fund deposited by me, the sum of a million, and charge the same to my account.
"One, two, three, four, five," said Monte Cristo; "five millions-why what a Croesus you are!"
"It is really wonderful," said the count; "above all, if, as I suppose, it is payable at sight."
"You do not doubt it?"
"No!"
"No," said Monte Cristo folding the five notes, "most decidedly not; the thing is so curious, I will make the experiment myself.
I will take the five scraps of paper that I now hold as bonds, with your signature alone, and here is a receipt in full for the six millions between us.
"What," he stammered, "do you mean to keep that money?
Excuse me, excuse me, but I owe this money to the charity fund,--a deposit which I promised to pay this morning."
"Certainly," he said, "your receipt is money."
"Pardon me, count, pardon me."
"Then I may keep this money?"
"Yes," said Danglars, while the perspiration started from the roots of his hair.
"Yes, keep it-keep it."
Monte Cristo replaced the notes in his pocket with that indescribable expression which seemed to say, "Come, reflect; if you repent there is still time."
"No," said Danglars, "no, decidedly no; keep my signatures.
How absurd-as if one crown were not as good as another.
"Certainly, I excuse you," said Monte Cristo graciously, "and pocket them." And he placed the bonds in his pocket book.
"But," said Danglars, "there is still a sum of one hundred thousand francs?"
"The balance would come to about that sum; but keep it, and we shall be quits."
At the door he found his carriage, and was immediately driven to the bank.
"Good morning, creditor," said he; "for I wager anything it is the creditor who visits me."
"Poor things!"
"Yes."
"I have brought my receipt."
"Yes; well?"
"How so?"
My funds are deposited there, and you can understand that if I draw out ten millions on the same day it will appear rather strange to the governor.
Two days will be a different thing," said Danglars, smiling.
"Come," said Boville, with a tone of entire incredulity, "five millions to that gentleman who just left, and who bowed to me as though he knew me?"
"Here is his receipt.
"It is one of the best houses in Europe," said Danglars, carelessly throwing down the receipt on his desk.
Why, this Count of Monte Cristo must be a nabob?"
"I must visit him," he said, "and obtain some pious grant from him."
"Oh, you may make sure of him; his charities alone amount to twenty thousand francs a month."
"It is magnificent!
"What example?"
"They gave all their fortune to the hospitals."
"For what reason?"
"Because they would not spend money so guiltily acquired."
"Well, I must confess, these are scruples."
"I registered their deed of gift yesterday."
"And how much did they possess?"
"Oh, not much-from twelve to thirteen hundred thousand francs.
But to return to our millions."
"Are you then pressed for this money?"
"Yes; for the examination of our cash takes place to morrow."
"To morrow?
Why did you not tell me so before?
Why, it is as good as a century!
"At two o'clock."
"Send at twelve," said Danglars, smiling.
"Certainly; it will only cost you a discount of five thousand or six thousand francs." The receiver started back.
What a proposition!"
"And if that were the case it would be worth while to make some sacrifice."
"Thank you, no, sir."
"Then it will be to morrow."
"Yes; but without fail."
"I will come myself."
"No," said the banker; "I have appeared rather ridiculous since that affair of Benedetto, so I remain in the background."
"Listen-when one bears an irreproachable name, as I do, one is rather sensitive."
"no"
"I hate them for being poor.
It's the ugliest thing in the world.
Aren't people horrible!"
Never before in his life had Amory considered poor people.
Question.--Well-what's the situation?
Answer.--That I have about twenty four dollars to my name.
To morrow I'm going to leave New York for good.
It's a bad town unless you're on top of it.
I don't want to commit moral suicide.
I'm not sure.
Youth is like having a big plate of candy.
They don't.
The matron doesn't want to repeat her girlhood-she wants to repeat her honeymoon.
One Hundred and Twenty seventh Street-or One Hundred and Thirty seventh Street....
Two and three look alike-no, not much.
Seat damp... are clothes absorbing wetness from seat, or seat absorbing dryness from clothes?...
What?
That must have been One Hundred and Twelfth back there.
One O Two instead of One Two Seven.
Apartments along here expensive-probably hundred and fifty a month-maybe two hundred.
What a dirty river-want to go down there and see if it's dirty-French rivers all brown or black, so were Southern rivers.
He could live on it three months and sleep in the park.
Wonder where Jill was-Jill Bayne, Fayne, Sayne-what the devil-neck hurts, darned uncomfortable seat.
No desire to sleep with Jill, what could Alec see in her?
Alec had a coarse taste in women.
Own taste the best; Isabelle, Clara, Rosalind, Eleanor, were all American. Eleanor would pitch, probably southpaw.
Rosalind was outfield, wonderful hitter, Clara first base, maybe.
Where's the darned bell-
A man approached through the heavy gloom.
"Got a pass?"
Is this private?"
"This is the Hudson River Sporting and Yacht Club."
I'm just resting."
"Well-" began the man dubiously.
"I'll go if you want me to."

He knew that he could sophisticate himself finally into saying that his own weakness was just the result of circumstances and environment; that often when he raged at himself as an egotist something would whisper ingratiatingly: "no
Genius!"
He shivered.
Amory smiled a bit.
And again-
"Stop worrying-"
He fancied a possible future comment of his own.
They were too easy, too dangerous to the public mind.

He found something that he wanted, had always wanted and always would want-not to be admired, as he had feared; not to be loved, as he had made himself believe; but to be necessary to people, to be indispensable; he remembered the sense of security he had found in Burne.
